channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: He’s the Last Great Land Artist You’ve Never Heard Of
item.description: Charles Ross spent 50 years building “Star Axis,” a naked-eye observatory in New Mexico. Now his masterwork is ready. How to share it with a changed world?
item.dc:creator: Travis Diehl and Brad Trone
item.category: Los Angeles County Museum of Art
item.media:credit: Charles Ross/Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York
item.media:description: The Milky Way ribbons through the sky above “Star Axis.”

item.title: Oskar Eustis Is Stepping Down From the Public Theater
item.description: Eustis has been the Public Theater’s artistic director for 21 years, stewarding Free Shakespeare in the Park, nurturing “Hamilton” and navigating the coronavirus pandemic.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:description: “I did not think remaking the business model in the midst of the greatest crisis in the history of the American theater was going to be my lot,” Oskar Eustis said.

item.title: Six Years After a Reckoning, Criterion Puts ‘Cinematic Blackness’ in Focus
item.description: Adding a curator, the Criterion Collection expanded its offerings and its vision of Black film. But auteurs continue to push the company on what inclusion means.
item.dc:creator: Beandrea July
item.media:credit: First row, from left: University City Studios; and Film at Lincoln Center. Second row, from left: Michael Ochs Archives/Getty Images; and Janus Films. Third row, from left: Amazon Studios; and Michael Ochs Archives, via Getty Images

item.title: Kenneth Blume Steps Out of Kenny Beats’s Shadow
item.description: The hitmaker formerly known as Kenny Beats was behind breakthrough albums for Geese, Vince Staples, Idles and Fcukers. On a new solo project, he turns his focus inward.
item.dc:creator: Reggie Ugwu
item.media:credit: Morganne Boulden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Blume wrote lyrics for his new solo album after an emotionally battering period of caring for his ill father. “The music feels like a safe place to get everything I want to say out,” he said.

item.title: Marlboro Music: Where Artists Have Time to Chase Perfection
item.description: The Marlboro Music Festival, now in its 75th summer, allows artists to practice for however long they’d like, with no pressure to perform.
item.dc:creator: David Weininger
item.media:credit: Graham Dickie for The New York Times
item.media:description: Visitors to the Marlboro Music Festival are greeted by this sign, one of many playful elements on its campus.

item.title: This French Ballet Star Wants You to Know He Really Cares
item.description: Hugo Marchand, an étoile at Paris Opera Ballet, brings his intensity and some high-wattage friends to New York City Center’s Artists at the Center.
item.dc:creator: Marina Harss
item.media:credit: Julien Mignot for The New York Times

item.title: Fire Ravaged the Glasgow School of Art. It May Cost Too Much to Restore It.
item.description: Designed by Charles Rennie Mackintosh, the Glasgow School of Art is one of Britain’s most famous buildings. The school says it can’t undertake a planned “faithful restoration” alone.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: The fire that destroyed the Glasgow School of Art building in 2018, four years after another huge fire there.

item.title: ‘The Vampire Lestat’ Was a Dissonant Viewing Experience
item.description: This season of “The Vampire Lestat” featured an outstanding lead performance but an unrecognizable title character.
item.dc:creator: Maya Phillips

item.title: Tate Museums Taps Leader of Dia Art Foundation as Next Director
item.description: After leading the Dia Art Foundation for over a decade, Jessica Morgan is returning to London to run Tate at a challenging time for the renowned group of art museums.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: Jessica Morgan in 2024. The Tate post is a homecoming for Morgan, who was born in London.

item.title: Former Conductor to Plead Guilty in Child Sex Abuse Imagery Case
item.description: Julian Wachner, the former music director at Trinity Church in New York, faces up to 10 years in prison after reaching a plea deal.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:credit: Vincent Tullo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Julian Wachner directed the music program for Trinity Church in New York until his ouster in 2022.

item.title: New Poster for ‘La La Land’ Fixes Ryan Gosling’s Wayward Hand
item.description: Gosling’s hand position during a dance scene didn’t match Emma Stone’s, haunting him for years after the movie’s release.
item.dc:creator: Malia Mendez
item.media:description: Justin Manfredi, an executive vice president for Lionsgate, marveled with a hint of jest at how “vastly different” a revised “La La Land” poster was from its predecessor.

item.title: Whitney Cummings’s Trump Joke at Kennedy Center Is Cut From Netflix Special
item.description: At the Mark Twain Prize ceremony, the comedian Whitney Cummings made a joke about the president that seemed to refer to Jeffrey Epstein.
item.dc:creator: Malia Mendez
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:description: Whitney Cummings opened the ceremony for the Mark Twain Prize, which was awarded to Bill Maher at the Kennedy Center last month.

item.title: G.O.P. Lawmakers Join White House in Challenging a Smithsonian Museum
item.description: A House subcommittee questioned the director of the National Museum of American History after a scathing White House report characterized it as insufficiently patriotic.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin
item.media:credit: Valerie Plesch for The New York Times
item.media:description: Republican lawmakers were harshly critical of Anthea M. Hartig, director of the Smithsonian’s National Museum of American History, during a hearing on Tuesday. Their Democratic colleagues dismissed the allegations as unfounded.

item.title: Cristo Fernandez Talks Returning to Pro Soccer After “Ted Lasso”
item.description: Cristo Fernandez was a soccer player before he was an actor. After finding TV stardom, he’s making the unlikely return to his first career.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.media:credit: Paul Ratje for The New York Times
item.media:description: Cristo Fernandez recently made his first appearance for El Paso Locomotive FC in the second tier of men’s professional soccer in the United States.

item.title: Late Night Rags on Trump and His Cabinet Joining TikTok
item.description: “Right now TikTok is, like, ‘Oh, no, are we the new Facebook?’” Jimmy Fallon said on “The Tonight Show.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Peter Lassally, Longtime Late-Night TV Producer, Dies at 93
item.description: He was known as the “host whisperer” for his success at producing shows starring Johnny Carson, David Letterman and Craig Ferguson.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.category: Late Show With David Letterman (TV Program)
The Late Late Show with Craig Ferguson (TV Program)
item.media:credit: J. Emilio Flores for The New York Times
item.media:description: Peter Lassally in an undated photo. One of his strengths was “getting the host to understand how to listen,” the comedian Garry Shandling said, and “how to ask the questions that an audience might want to hear.”

item.title: Doreen Broadnax, Famous (as Sparky D) for ’80s Rap Battle, Dies at 61
item.description: Tossing verbal grenades back and forth with Roxanne Shanté, she helped establish a lasting hip-hop truism: Bad blood is good business.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:description: Doreen Broadnax in 1988, after the end of the so-called Roxanne Wars, one of the first rap battles.

item.title: Jason Alexander Apologizes for 2012 Sketch With Teenage Courtney Stodden
item.description: Alexander rubs a phone against the actress’s breasts in the Funny or Die comedy sketch, which was published when she was 17.
item.dc:creator: Reggie Ugwu
item.media:description: “I completely agree that it was inappropriate and I truly regret it,” Jason Alexander said.

item.title: They Wrote a Picture Book to Encourage Bipartisanship
item.description: Ellie Gottheimer and Larra Mullin, who met when their fathers were in Congress, make a case for bipartisanship in a picture book about dogs working together.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Inspired by their fathers’ friendship, Ellie Gottheimer, left, and Larra Mullin wrote “Shmoo & Ozzie Go to Washington.”

item.title: Ayo Edebiri and Ebon Moss-Bachrach on ‘The Bear,’ Family Recipes and More
item.description: Watch Ayo Edebiri and Ebon Moss-Bachrach make a family recipe for vegetarian borscht.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Chen
item.media:credit: Taylor Miller for The New York Times

item.title: The Good List: 6 Things to Bring Joy and Delight to Your Day
item.description: Forgetting yourself, listening to forests and The Most ‘Wuthering Heights’ Day Ever.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Sony Revives Cinerama Dome in Los Angeles as Box Office Sales Rebound
item.description: The iconic Cinerama Dome and adjacent 14-screen theater complex will reopen in 2028 after a renovation. The venue closed in 2020 because of the pandemic.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.category: Cinerama Dome (Los Angeles, Calif, Movie Theater)
item.media:credit: Kate Warren for The New York Times
item.media:description: The 956-seat Cinerama Dome in Hollywood first opened in 1963.

item.title: ‘White Nights’: A Loner’s All-Consuming Crush
item.description: Immaculately realized and restored, Luchino Visconti’s adaptation of Dostoyevsky’s short story about a chance encounter on a bridge arrives at Film Forum.
item.dc:creator: J. Hoberman
item.media:description: Marcello Mastroianni, right, and Maria Schell in Luchino Visconti’s “White Nights.”

item.title: ‘Fast Charlie,’ ‘Lucky Grandma’ and More Streaming Gems
item.description: Skip the endless scrolling with these seven overlooked movie recommendations.
item.dc:creator: Jason T. Bailey

item.title: ‘The Daily Show’ Tackles Trump’s Latest Move in His ‘War on Canada’
item.description: “Fighting again with Canada?” Desi Lydic said about the president imposing new tariffs on the country. “I thought we were going to work this ‘oot.’”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Plas Johnson, Saxophonist Behind ‘The Pink Panther,’ Dies at 94
item.description: His recording for the theme song, written by Henry Mancini, included a solo improvised in the studio. He also backed Frank Sinatra and Barbra Streisand.
item.dc:creator: Matt Schudel

item.title: The Last Song That Made Me Cry, and 4 Other Recommendations
item.description: Listen to tracks from Phoebe Bridgers, Kelela, L7 and more.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:description: Phoebe Bridgers

item.title: Kaylee Hottle, ‘Godzilla vs. Kong’ Actress, Dies in Car Crash
item.description: A deaf teenage actress, she appeared in “Godzilla vs. Kong” and “Godzilla x Kong: The New Empire.”
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather
item.category: Godzilla x Kong: The New Empire (Movie)
item.media:credit: Frederic J. Brown/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Kaylee Hottle at the premiere of “Godzilla x Kong: The New Empire” in Hollywood, Calif., in March 2024.

item.title: Europe Cancels Venice Biennale Funding Over Russia’s Return
item.description: The European authorities had warned the organizers of the major art show for months that they would lose funding if Russia took part.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Casey Kelbaugh for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Russian pavilion at the Venice Biennale this year.

item.title: Ava DuVernay’s Documentary ’14th’ Will Close New York Film Festival
item.description: Described as a “sequel of sorts” to “13TH,” the new movie argues that the 14th Amendment, which guarantees birthright citizenship, is under attack.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Goodman
item.media:description: Ava DuVernay, left, with the former librarian of Congress, Carla Hayden.

item.title: Like It or Not, Summer Means Storytime at Ballet Theater
item.description: The company wrapped up its Met season of safe war horses with multiple debuts and a slew of promotions, including the onstage appointment of three principals.
item.dc:creator: Gia Kourlas

item.title: ‘Les Misérables’ in Concert and More: A Show’s Never-Ending Life
item.description: Concert versions and touring productions keep the spirit of 19th-century French revolutionaries alive.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli

item.title: Bill Maher Feuded With Trump. Did He Get the Last Laugh?
item.description: How the late-night host won comedy’s biggest prize — despite the White House’s objections.
item.dc:creator: Benoit Denizet-Lewis
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
Real Time With Bill Maher (TV Program)
item.media:credit: Emily Shur for The New York Times

item.title: Hot, Uncomfortable, Transcendent: Why We Can’t Quit Opera at Bayreuth
item.description: The Bayreuth Festival, where Wagner’s operas have been performed for 150 years, is inconvenient and unpleasant. Why does it have such a hold on us?
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone

item.title: 7 Other ‘Odyssey’ Movie Adaptations to Stream Now
item.description: For decades, filmmakers have been borrowing plot lines and metaphors from Homer’s epic for adventures set in different eras and realities.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam

item.title: When Listening to Music Was a Team Sport
item.description: An exhibition at the Cooper Hewitt celebrates the glorious machines that gave us the soundtracks of our lives before streaming sites shoved them toward obsolescence.
item.dc:creator: Walker Mimms

item.title: Seth Meyers Calls Trump ‘The Sports Reaper’
item.description: The late-night host joked that the president’s sports curse had struck again after he backed Lionel Messi of Argentina, which then lost the World Cup final to Spain.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: White House Adds Jefferson Statue to the Rose Garden
item.description: The statue, which depicts the third president drafting the Declaration of Independence, joins those of Washington, Franklin and Hamilton.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin

item.title: Dave Dawson, Co-Founder of Bespoke Lighting Company Urban Electric, Dies at 53
item.description: He and his wife knew nothing about the business when they decided to produce lighting in Charleston, S.C. Their company became a beacon of American manufacturing.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green

item.title: As Budget Cuts Loom, French Theater Turns Up the Political Heat
item.description: This year’s Avignon Festival has transformed into a staging ground for protests, defiant new works and some early 2027 presidential campaigning.
item.dc:creator: Laura Cappelle
item.media:description: Julia Bernat, Danilo Grangheia and Wagner Moura in “A Trial — after An Enemy of the People” at the Avignon Festival.

item.title: Greece Hopes Andy Burnham Will Return the Parthenon Sculptures
item.description: The artworks, also known as the Parthenon marbles, once decorated the renowned ancient temple in Athens, Greece. They have been displayed in the British Museum in London for decades.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: The Parthenon Sculptures on display at the British Museum in London in 2023.

item.title: The World Cup Halftime Show Had Star Power. But the Style Missed the Point.
item.description: Most performers for the halftime show, including BTS, Gustavo Dudamel and Madonna, dressed for the occasion but didn’t hit the mark.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.media:description: Madonna, in her Saint Laurent warm-up look, performing with the Brazilian soccer player Ronaldinho, left, and the Brazilian former soccer player Ronaldo during the halftime show on Sunday.

item.title: Here’s What Greeks Think of Christopher Nolan’s ‘The Odyssey’
item.description: Before its release, some in the country were highly skeptical about the new depiction of a beloved tale. But many who saw it over opening weekend came back with positive reviews.
item.dc:creator: Niki Kitsantonis

item.title: D4vd Murder Case: Prosecutors Share Photos and More Evidence During Preliminary Hearing
item.description: At a hearing, witnesses have described a foul odor emanating from the singer’s vehicle. He is accused of fatally stabbing Celeste Rivas Hernandez and dismembering her body.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:description: The singer D4vd was arraigned in April and charged with first-degree murder.

item.title: ‘Heated Rivalry,’ ‘Dukes,’ With Jane Wickline, and More Must-See Theater
item.description: As the summer heats up, our critics are raving about these seven not-to-miss Off Broadway shows.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw and Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.category: Heated Rivalry: The Unauthorized Musical Parody (Play)
Goodbye Horses: An Unauthorized Retelling of Jonathan Demme's "The Silence of the Lambs" (Play)
What a World! What a World! (Play)

item.title: The Nerves and Thrills of Being a ‘Swan Lake’ Extra
item.description: Our reporter, a lapsed dancer, got an intimate glimpse of the action in the American Ballet Theater’s production.
item.dc:creator: Margaret Fuhrer and Paola Chapdelaine

item.title: Cat Cohen Turned Her Stroke Into a Punchline and Off Broadway Show
item.description: “Broad Strokes” recounts this singing comedian’s medical emergency, juxtaposing “something brash and grotesque and humiliating with something completely heartfelt.”
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Gellman and Tess Mayer
item.media:description: For Cohen, there is pathos in playing silly moments off the sincere.

item.title: ‘National Treasure,’ the Over-Engineered, Delightfully Dumb U.S. History Lesson
item.description: Nicolas Cage, Diane Kruger and Justin Bartha raced from conspiracy theory to conspiracy theory in this 2004 blockbuster about stealing the Declaration of Independence.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam

item.title: To Sell Homer’s ‘Odyssey,’ Book Publishers Get Creative
item.description: The market for the epic poem is as crowded as a certain Ithacan palace, and publishers have had to get crafty to attract readers to their editions.
item.dc:creator: MJ Franklin

item.title: Cinephiles Took Their Own Journeys to See ‘The Odyssey’ in IMAX 70mm
item.description: To see Christopher Nolan’s interpretation of an epic journey in his preferred IMAX 70mm format, admirers packed their bags and took out their passports.
item.dc:creator: Malia Mendez

item.title: ‘Ransom Canyon,’ and 5 More Shows to Watch on TV This Week
item.description: The second season of the cowboy drama airs. And a documentary shows the behind the scenes of the “Heartstopper: Forever” movie.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:description: From left: Josh Duhamel and Jack Schumacher in “Ransom Canyon.”

item.title: ‘House of the Dragon’ Season 3, Episode 5 Recap: Under Pressure
item.description: Queen Rhaenrya is beginning to sound a little paranoid. That rarely bodes well with an autocrat.
item.dc:creator: Sean T. Collins
item.media:description: Rhaenyra (Emma D’Arcy, right) is right when she tells Daemon (Matt Smith) there is a mastermind behind the violence in the city. What’s worrisome is how she talks about it.

item.title: First World Cup Halftime Show: Lots of Stars, Little Purpose
item.description: The performers valiantly attempted to justify the existence of the show, which was impeccably choreographed and thematically jumbled.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:description: The first World Cup halftime show had a simple message of love and unity.

item.title: Burna Boy is one of the biggest stars to emerge from Africa.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:description: Burna Boy performing with Shakira at the World Cup opening ceremony in June.

item.title: Gustavo Dudamel will lead a tribute to victims in Venezuela.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:description: Gustavo Dudamel will unite the New York Philharmonic and the Simón Bolívar Symphony Orchestra of Venezuela during his performance.

item.title: Justin Bieber’s year has been filled with high-profile performances.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:description: Justin Bieber at the Grammys in February.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Emmanuel Morgan and Elena Bergeron

item.title: Shakira’s ‘Dai Dai’ is the official anthem of the tournament.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:credit: Eduardo Verdugo/Associated Press, via Ap Photo/Eduardo Verdugo
item.media:description: Shakira performing in Mexico City before the first game of the World Cup in June.

item.title: Madonna, a veteran of big stages, just released a new album.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:description: Madonna performs on a platform overlooking Times Square in June.

item.title: BTS brings K-pop to a global stage.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:description: BTS performs at the group’s comeback concert this year.

item.title: ‘The Odyssey’ Brings in $124.5 Million at the Box Office, Powered by Christopher Nolan
item.description: Christopher Nolan’s nearly three-hour epic was No. 1 over the weekend, with about $124.5 million in ticket sales in the United States and Canada.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes

item.title: How Kerry Washington and Whitney White Are Honoring Whoopi Goldberg Through Theater
item.description: The actress Kerry Washington and the director Whitney White want to honor Goldberg’s theatrical legacy by revisiting her acclaimed portraits of humanity.
item.dc:creator: Salamishah Tillet
item.media:credit: Marcus Maddox for The New York Times
item.media:description: The director Whitney White and the actress Kerry Washington collaborated on “The Whoopi Monologues,” at Lincoln Center Theater.

item.title: Joe Melson, 91, Dies; Wrote ‘Only the Lonely’ and Other Hits With Roy Orbison
item.description: Working together closely, the pair turned out more than 200 songs, including “Crying,” “Running Scared” and “Blue Bayou.”
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
